summary: Jeff Atwood responds to Wil Shipley's criticism that Windows users don't support independent software developers by declaring 'Support Your Favorite Small Software Vendor Day.' He acknowledges that 90% of Windows software is crap but argues that Sturgeon's Law applies to all platforms. Atwood confesses to using Media Monkey extensively without paying and rectifies this by registering it. He challenges Windows users to find software they rely on daily and actually pay for it, arguing that professional developers should be willing to spend $20 a month supporting independent developers.
key_points: Wil Shipley claims Windows users don't believe in the 'little guy' and prefer safe, market-leading choices over quality indie software
Atwood concedes that 90% of Windows software is crap but notes this applies to all media per Sturgeon's Law
He declares December 1st, 2006 as 'Support Your Favorite Small Software Vendor Day' to counter negative stereotypes about Windows users
Professional developers should be able to spend $20/month supporting independent software creators whose tools they use daily
Supporting indie developers is both an obligation to them and to ourselves as users who benefit from their work
money_quotes: 90% of Windows software is absolute unfettered crap which should never be installed on any computer running any operating system. Ever.
But our 90% is larger than your 90%.
Let's support the little guy who still gives a damn about creating small, beautiful, useful apps on an operating system that gets no respect.
Stop reading this post right now and buy that software.

If I can't come up with the scratch to spend a measly $20 a month supporting the very best work of my fellow independent software developers, can I really call myself a professional software developer?
I believe in the little guy writing cool Windows software. And by 'believe in,' I mean 'pay.'
key_insight: Believing in independent software developers means actually paying for their software, not just using and appreciating it.
